text: Laparoscopic Chimera Duodenum Duodenostomy for Duodenal Atresia. Our patient is a five-day-old male newborn with a past medical history of birth at thirty-seven weeks gestation via c-section of a thirty-six-year-old female with a pregnancy without complications. the weight at birth is two point nine kilograms and length is forty five centimeters postnatally our patient was diagnosed with down syndrome and a systolic murmur chief complaint was intolerance to oral feeding since birth and bilious vomiting patient was treated in a primary care hospital with orogastic discompression electrolyte and fluid reposition and referred to our tertiary care pediatric hospital

Preoperative studies were obtained with a plain abdominal x-ray which showed important dilatation of gastric chamber and a double bubble sign. Upper GI series with hydrosoluble contrast demonstrated an obstruction at the duodenum. Bulbulus with intestinal malrotation was excluded with a colon enema.

Diagnosis of duodenum atresia or stenosis was made and surgery proposed. Pediatric cardiology department diagnosed pattern ductus arteriosus and a ventricular sept defect with no hemodynamic repercussion and no surgical contraindication. Informed consent was obtained and surgery undertaken.

The patient is positioned supine with the table in reverse Trendelenburg and frog-legged, secured with strappings. A 5mm trocar is inserted at the infraumbilical fold using an open technique, followed by the placement of a 30-degree telescope. Two additional laparoscopic ports are inserted: one below the ribcage at the midclavicular line and another at the right midclavicular line above the umbilicus. All trocars are sutured to the body wall with rubber rings, and pneumoperitoneum is maintained at 5-8 mmHg.

During laparoscopy, the liver is retracted using a percutaneous stay suture in the falciform ligament, with an additional suture at the right hepatic lobe for better exposure. The surgeon excludes intestinal malrotation under laparoscopic vision. The duodenum is identified, and the right colon is mobilized at the hepatic flexure, revealing the dilated upper duodenum and the inferior duodenum. A gentle dissection is performed, and the jejunum is freed using a laparoscopic Kocher maneuver.

The surgeon suspends the upper end of the duodenum using a percutaneous suture to facilitate distal dissection. A transverse incision of 1.5 centimeters is made in the upper duodenum using laparoscopic scissors held in the left hand. The orogastric tube is advanced by the anesthesiologist under laparoscopic guidance to check for duodenal webs. The inferior duodenum is incised longitudinally with the same length to ensure proper caliber and patency for the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an Akimura diamond and stemosis using polydioxanone sutures. The first suture is placed at the left corner of the transverse incision, extending to the midpoint of the longitudinal incision in the distal duodenum. Both ends of the thread are exteriorized percutaneously. The same technique is applied to the right side of the incisions. The suspension of the upper duodenum is loosened to achieve a more physiological anastomosis. The posterior side of the anastomosis is completed with extracorporeal laparoscopic interrupted knots starting at the midline, while the anterior side follows the same process. Lateral knots are tied intracorporeally.

The surgeon tests the patency and impermeability of the duodenal anastomosis by injecting 20 milliliters of saline solution and 20 milliliters of air through the orogastric tube. A closed drain is placed and exteriorized through the five-millimeter port. The orogastric tube remains in place to decompress the stomach and monitor motility.

In the postoperative period there was a complication due to pneumonia. A day postoperative six upper GI series was made to prove patency of anastomosis and absence of leaks. Enteral feedings through orogastric tube was started and patient was discharged after antibiotic treatment.
